summary:
Lifting, Moving & Positioning

Back injuries end EMS careers. Body mechanics that protect you:

  • Lift with your legs, back straight — never bend at the waist.
  • Keep the weight close — at arm's length it loads the spine ~10×.
  • Wide base (feet shoulder-width); never twist — pivot your whole body.
  • Call "ready, lift" to move together; get more help rather than overload.

Types of move

  • Emergency move (fire, explosion, HazMat, unsafe scene): drag them to safety NOW — spinal precautions are abandoned. Clothes drag, blanket drag, firefighter's carry.
  • Urgent move (critical patient, possible spine injury, no scene danger): rapid extrication with manual spinal stabilization maintained throughout (<1 min).
  • Non-urgent move (no threat): controlled handling with full precautions as indicated.

Devices

Wheeled cot (always strap the patient), stair chair (stairs/narrow halls, 2+ EMS providers), scoop stretcher (splits to slide under — great for awkward positions), long board (full spinal immobilization with collar + head blocks), basket/Stokes (rough-terrain rescue). Strap chest, hips, knees; pad voids.

Positions

  • Supine — default assessment / CPR.
  • Fowler's / semi-Fowler's (sitting up) — respiratory distress.
  • Recovery (left lateral) — unconscious, breathing, with a pulse: lets fluids drain.
  • Pregnancy (3rd trimester): tilt left (wedge under the right hip) to keep the uterus off the vena cava.

Trendelenburg is no longer routine for shock. Bariatric patients need rated equipment and extra hands; pediatric and geriatric patients need padding and gentle handling.

quiz.q: Which of the following is the PRIMARY cause of career-ending injuries in EMS?
When performing an emergency move, what is the priority?
options: Maintain spinal immobilization at all costs
Remove the patient from immediate danger as quickly as possible

quiz.q: What is the correct body position when lifting a patient?
options: Keep your legs straight and bend at the waist
Keep your back straight, feet shoulder-width apart, and lift with your legs
Twist at the waist to reach the patient
Keep your feet together to maintain balance

quiz.q: What position should a patient with severe shortness of breath be placed in?
Rapid extrication from a vehicle is an example of what type of move?
Which device is designed to split into two halves to be placed under a patient with minimal movement?
When moving a patient down a flight of stairs, which device is most appropriate?
The recovery position (left lateral recumbent) is indicated for:
options: Unconscious patients who are breathing with a pulse

quiz.q: How should a bariatric patient be lifted?
options: With two EMS providers using standard equipment
With additional personnel, bariatric-rated equipment, and mechanical lifts if available

quiz.q: What is the proper position for a pregnant patient in the third trimester, and why?
options: Supine with a wedge under the right hip, to prevent supine hypotensive syndrome
Left lateral recumbent, to reduce back pain

quiz.q: What is the most important safety consideration when using a wheeled stretcher?
options: Always securing the patient with safety straps
Using the stretcher only for critical patients
Ensuring the stretcher is folded before loading

quiz.q: If you need to twist while carrying a patient, what should you do?
options: Pivot with your entire body — do not twist your spine
Twist quickly to minimize time in an awkward position
Twist only if you are the stronger EMS provider

flashcards.question: What is the most important rule of body mechanics when lifting?
flashcards.answer: Lift with your legs, not your back — use the large muscle groups of the legs to do the work.

flashcards.question: When are emergency moves indicated?
flashcards.answer: When the patient or rescuer is in immediate danger (fire, hazardous environment, unsafe scene). Spinal precautions are temporarily abandoned.

flashcards.question: What is an example of an emergency move?
flashcards.answer: The clothes drag — grasping the patient's clothing at the neck and dragging them to safety.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between urgent and non-urgent moves?
flashcards.answer: Urgent moves are for serious conditions without immediate danger (rapid extrication with spinal precautions). Non-urgent moves are for stable patients with no threat.

flashcards.question: What position is best for a patient in respiratory distress?
flashcards.answer: Fowler's or semi-Fowler's position (sitting upright 45-90 degrees) to maximize diaphragmatic excursion.

flashcards.question: What position should an unconscious patient with adequate breathing be placed in?
flashcards.answer: Left lateral recumbent (recovery position) to protect the airway and allow fluids to drain.

flashcards.question: How should a pregnant patient in the third trimester be positioned?
flashcards.answer: Supine with a wedge under the right hip to displace the uterus off the inferior vena cava (left lateral displacement).

flashcards.question: What is the power lift?
flashcards.answer: A lifting technique from a squatting position with a straight back, feet flat, and arms close to the body, using leg muscles to lift.

critical.scenario: You and your partner respond to a single-vehicle rollover MVC. The car is resting on its roof, and smoke is coming from the engine compartment. The driver, a 45-year-old male, is conscious, confused, and still buckled in, hanging upside down. He has a visible scalp laceration with moderate bleeding. The smoke is starting to thicken.
critical.question: What type of move should be performed, and why?
options: Non-urgent move — take time to apply a cervical collar and log-roll the patient onto a backboard
Emergency move — there is an immediate fire risk; rapidly remove the patient from the vehicle using a clothes drag or similar technique, abandoning spinal precautions
Wait for the fire department to extinguish any potential fire before moving the patient
Perform rapid extrication (urgent move) with spinal precautions since the bleeding is only moderate

critical.scenario: You are treating a 72-year-old female with a history of COPD who called 911 for severe respiratory distress. She is sitting in a chair at home, gasping for air, with an SpO2 of 82% on room air. She is awake but can only speak in 2-word sentences. Her breathing is labored with accessory muscle use. You need to move her to the ambulance for transport.
critical.question: Which position and move are most appropriate for this patient?
options: Place her supine on the stretcher for spinal precautions
Have her walk to the ambulance to maintain her dignity
Assist her into a Fowler's position on the stair chair, keep her upright during transport on the stretcher, and administer oxygen continuously
Place her in Trendelenburg to improve blood flow to the brain

critical.scenario: You are called to a second-floor apartment for a 58-year-old male who fell and cannot get up. He is alert, oriented, and complains of right hip pain. He cannot bear weight. You suspect a hip fracture. The apartment hallway is narrow, and there are two flights of stairs to the ambulance. The patient is approximately 250 lbs.
critical.question: What is your safest plan for moving this patient?
options: Have him hop down the stairs with your assistance
Use a stair chair with two EMS providers, secure the patient, pad the injured hip, and use a track system if available; call for additional personnel if needed
Use a long spine board and slide him down the stairs
Have him sit on the stairs and scoot down one step at a time
